skill.name: stripe-projects
skill.description: Provision third-party services with Stripe Projects, including catalog search, project initialization, service setup, and credential handling.
skill.summary: Set up cloud services and credentials through the Stripe Projects CLI.
skill.author: stripe

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Ruby backtick execution findings, but these are Markdown command examples and inline command references, not executable Ruby code. The skill is not malicious, but it legitimately invokes external Stripe and Homebrew commands, installs a CLI plugin, provisions third-party services, and handles environment variables, so publication should include an elevated-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: External Provisioning Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the agent to run Stripe CLI commands that can initialize projects, install the Projects plugin, search catalogs, and provision services. This is expected for the skill, but it can change external infrastructure and should require user awareness.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and align with the skill purpose. They are not hidden execution, but they can affect real third-party services.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Environment Variable Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill's purpose includes retrieving API keys or tokens and listing environment variables. It instructs agents to show only environment variable names in summaries, which reduces exposure, but direct credential workflows remain sensitive.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly describes credential retrieval and environment variable workflows. The same file also includes guardrails that limit accidental value disclosure.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick findings point to Markdown code fences and inline command examples. No executable Ruby file or runtime backtick execution is present in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected file is Markdown, and the flagged syntax is documentation formatting. The commands are visible examples rather than embedded Ruby execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Stripe CLI installation documentation. They are expected references and do not show credential exfiltration or suspicious network destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URLs use the official Stripe documentation domain. No sensitive data is appended to the URLs in the inspected content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Supported
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography alert at the frontmatter description line has no supporting cryptographic API, algorithm name, or encryption operation in the inspected file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 starts a Markdown frontmatter description block. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code or weak algorithm usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: CLI Plugin Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs installation of the Stripe Projects plugin. Plugin installation is a legitimate setup step, but it expands the trusted code surface.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The plugin installation command is explicit and central to the workflow. No obfuscation or malicious destination is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Skill Handoff
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill checks for and invokes a locally installed stripe-projects-cli skill after project initialization. This is documented behavior, but it depends on local files created by the CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The handoff is clearly described and not hidden. It still relies on generated local skill content that was not part of this one-file scan.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time creating accounts, keys, and service configuration across many providers. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Stripe Projects workflows for catalog search, provisioning, and credential setup.
actual_capabilities: Checks whether the Stripe CLI is installed and available.
Installs or upgrades the Stripe CLI on macOS through Homebrew.
Installs the Stripe Projects CLI plugin.
Searches and browses the Stripe Projects service catalog.
Initializes a Stripe Projects workspace when needed.
Hands off to the local stripe-projects-cli skill after initialization.
limitations: Requires the Stripe CLI and Projects plugin to be installed.
May require browser authentication before provisioning can continue.
Can only provision services that appear in the Projects catalog.
Does not replace provider review, billing review, or security approval.

use_cases.title: Add a managed database
use_cases.description: Search the catalog, choose an available database provider, initialize the project, and prepare environment variables.

use_cases.title: Connect supporting infrastructure
use_cases.description: Find services for caching, email, search, queues, storage, monitoring, or feature flags and add them to a project.

use_cases.title: Review provisioned services
use_cases.description: Check project status, list provisioned resources, and summarize service names, tiers, and environment variable names.

prompt_templates.title: Browse services
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me which database services are available in Stripe Projects for this workspace.

prompt_templates.title: Set up a provider
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Stripe Projects to add a Postgres database for my app and show only the environment variable names.

prompt_templates.title: Compare service options
prompt_templates.prompt: Find available caching and queue providers in the Stripe Projects catalog, then recommend options for a production web app.

prompt_templates.title: Provision a service stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Initialize Stripe Projects if needed, add a database and observability service, then summarize provider, service type, tier, and env var names.

output_examples.input: I need Redis for this project.
output_examples.output: The assistant searches the Projects catalog, confirms available caching providers, initializes the project if needed, and summarizes the selected provider, service type, tier, and environment variable names.
The assistant checks project status through the Stripe Projects CLI and returns a concise list of provisioned services without exposing secret values.

output_examples.input: Add email sending and suggest related services.
output_examples.output: The assistant provisions an available email service, then suggests complementary catalog services such as observability, storage, or feature flags when present.

best_practices: Confirm the requested provider exists in the catalog before provisioning.
Show environment variable names only unless the user explicitly requests values.
Stop when browser authentication is required and wait for user confirmation.
anti_patterns: Do not fabricate provider names, service tiers, or commands.
Do not manually edit generated .projects or .env files.
Do not continue provisioning after authentication opens until the user confirms completion.

faq.question: What does this skill provision?
faq.answer: It provisions third-party services available through the Stripe Projects catalog, such as databases, caching, hosting, search, and monitoring.

faq.question: Does it expose secret values?
faq.answer: Its summary format says to show environment variable names only. Users should request and handle secret values carefully.

faq.question: Does it require Stripe CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow checks for Stripe CLI and installs or upgrades it on macOS when needed.

faq.question: Can it work without browser sign-in?
faq.answer: Some operations may require browser authentication. The workflow stops until the user completes sign-in.

faq.question: Can it use services outside the catalog?
faq.answer: No. The workflow instructs the assistant to stop or suggest alternatives when a service is not found.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it runs external commands, installs a plugin, provisions services, and handles credential workflows.
